meta.title: How DNS Works
meta.subtitle: The internet's address book, from name to number
meta.promise: Understand how a domain name becomes a server you can reach, step by step.
outcomes: Explain what DNS does in one sentence
Trace a lookup from name to IP
Explain why caching and TTL matter

c-name.name: Domain names
c-name.summary: Human-friendly labels that stand in for numeric addresses.

c-resolver.name: The resolver
c-resolver.summary: The helper that does the lookup work on your behalf.

c-hierarchy.name: The hierarchy
c-hierarchy.summary: Root, TLD, and authoritative servers each answer part of the name.

c-cache.name: Caching and TTL
c-cache.summary: Answers are remembered for a while to make the web fast.

g-dns.definition: Domain Name System: the service that turns names like example.com into IP addresses.

g-ip.definition: The numeric address of a machine on a network, like a phone number for a server.

g-resolver.definition: The DNS client (often run by your ISP) that finds the answer for you.

g-ttl.definition: Time To Live: how long a DNS answer may be cached before it must be looked up again.

g-ns.definition: A server that holds DNS records and answers questions about a domain.

chk-what.q: What does DNS turn a domain name into?
chk-what.why: DNS resolves a human-friendly name to the numeric IP address a computer needs to connect.

chk-cache.q: Why does DNS cache answers with a TTL?
options: To avoid repeating lookups and keep the web fast
chk-cache.why: Caching for the TTL avoids asking again for every visit, which makes browsing fast and reduces load.

chk-hier.q: Which server gives the final authoritative answer for example.com?
chk-hier.hint: Follow the chain to the end.
chk-hier.why: Root points to the TLD, the TLD points to the domain's authoritative nameserver, which holds the actual record.

d-flow.title: A DNS lookup
d-flow.code:
flowchart LR
  U[Your app] --> R[Resolver]
  R --> ROOT[Root server]
  ROOT --> TLD[.com server]
  TLD --> AUTH[Authoritative NS]
  AUTH --> R
  R --> U

modules.title: The idea
modules.summary: What DNS is and why it exists.

lessons.title: The internet's address book
lessons.summary: Computers talk using numbers, but people remember names. DNS is the address book that connects the two.

sections.what: DNS (Domain Name System) turns a name like example.com into an IP address a computer can connect to.
sections.why: People remember names; machines route by numbers. DNS bridges that gap so you never memorize IP addresses.
sections.how: When you type a name, a lookup finds the matching number before any page loads.
sections.connects: Every website visit, email, and app request starts with a DNS lookup.
sections.ifChanged: Without DNS you would have to type a numeric address for every site, and those numbers change over time.

lessons.analogy: DNS is a phone book: you know the person's name, and it finds their number so you can call.
lessons.teachBack: Explain DNS to a friend in one sentence, without using the word 'address'.

predict.question: You type wikipedia.org and press Enter. What does your computer need FIRST, before anything can load?
options: An IP address for the server
predict.reveal: An IP address. Nothing can be requested until DNS turns wikipedia.org into a number your computer can open a connection to. Only then does the browser ask that server for the page.

analogyPairs.from: A name in the phone book
Looking it up once, then remembering it
analogyPairs.to: Caching the answer for the TTL

lessons.misconception: DNS does not store or deliver web pages. It only maps a name to an address; the page itself comes from the web server in a separate step afterwards.

modules.title: The lookup
modules.summary: How a name becomes a number.

lessons.title: The resolver does the work
lessons.summary: You do not search the whole internet yourself. A resolver asks the right servers for you.

sections.what: A resolver is the DNS client that takes your question and returns the final answer.
sections.why: It hides a multi-step search behind one simple request.
sections.how: It asks a chain of servers until it gets the IP, then hands it back to your app.
sections.connects: Your operating system and browser hand names to the resolver, often run by your ISP or a service like 1.1.1.1.
sections.ifChanged: A slow or broken resolver makes every site feel slow or unreachable, even when the sites are fine.

lessons.example: Type example.com and your resolver returns something like 93.184.216.34 for the browser to connect to.

worked.title: Watch one lookup run
worked.intro: A fresh laptop on a new network types **wikipedia.org**. Field names are real DNS concepts; the values show one realistic run.

steps.label: The browser checks its own cache
steps.detail: Nothing there: this machine has never looked the name up.

steps.label: The OS hands the question to the resolver
steps.detail: The resolver (often your ISP's, or a public one like 1.1.1.1) now does all the work.

steps.label: The resolver asks a root server
steps.detail: Root servers do not know the answer, but they know who handles .org.

steps.label: The resolver asks the .org TLD server
steps.detail: One step closer: .org knows which nameservers speak for wikipedia.org.

steps.label: The authoritative nameserver answers
steps.detail: This server is run for the domain owner and holds the actual record.

steps.label: The answer flows back and is cached
steps.detail: The resolver and your OS both remember it for the TTL.

worked.outcome: The browser connects to 185.15.59.224 and only then requests the page. The whole chain usually takes well under 100 ms, and the next lookup is instant because of the cache.

lessons.activity: Run `dig wikipedia.org` (or use an online dig tool) and find the IP and the TTL column in the ANSWER section.

lessons.title: Root, TLD, authoritative
lessons.summary: No single server knows every name. The work is split across a hierarchy that each answer part of the question.

sections.what: The name is resolved in steps: root server, then the TLD server (.com), then the domain's authoritative nameserver.
sections.why: Splitting the job lets DNS scale to billions of names without one giant database.
sections.how: Each level points the resolver to the next, narrowing down until the authoritative server gives the record.
sections.connects: The authoritative nameserver is controlled by whoever owns the domain.
sections.ifChanged: If a level is misconfigured, names under it stop resolving until it is fixed.

lessons.analogy: It is like finding a house: country, then city, then street, then the exact address.
lessons.activity: Run `dig example.com +trace` (or an online DNS trace) and watch it walk root to authoritative.

analogyPairs.to: Root server (knows who handles each TLD)

predict.question: A root server goes offline right now. What do you notice while browsing?
predict.reveal: Nothing noticeable. There are 13 root server identities served by hundreds of machines around the world (anycast), and resolvers cache the root's referrals anyway. The system is built so no single machine matters.

lessons.title: Caching and TTL
lessons.summary: Looking up every name every time would be slow. DNS remembers answers for a while.

sections.what: Resolvers cache answers for a period called the TTL (time to live).
sections.why: Most lookups are repeats, so caching removes almost all of the round trips.
sections.how: An answer carries a TTL; until it expires the resolver reuses the cached value.
sections.connects: TTL is set on DNS records by the domain owner, trading freshness against speed.
sections.ifChanged: A very long TTL makes changes take longer to appear; a very short TTL increases lookups.

lessons.warning: When you change where a domain points, users may keep hitting the old value until the TTL expires.
lessons.teachBack: Explain the tradeoff a short TTL versus a long TTL in your own words.

scenario.title: Play the resolver
scenario.prompt: Same browser, three situations. Pick one and watch what actually happens.

choices.label: You revisit a site from a minute ago
steps: The name is still in the cache and the TTL has not expired
No DNS server is contacted at all
choices.outcome: Instant answer from cache. This is why the second visit always feels faster.

choices.label: You typo a domain that does not exist
steps: The resolver walks root and TLD as usual
The authoritative side replies NXDOMAIN (no such name)
The resolver caches this negative answer too
choices.outcome: You get a lookup error, and repeat attempts fail instantly because even 'no' is cached for a while.

choices.label: The site moved servers an hour ago
steps: Your resolver still holds the old IP because its TTL has not expired
Traffic keeps going to the old address
When the TTL runs out, the next lookup fetches the new IP
choices.outcome: You may reach the old server until the TTL expires. This is why admins lower the TTL before a migration.

lessons.activity: Run `dig` on any site twice, a few seconds apart, and watch the TTL number count down between runs.
